title: Iran-US Negotiations: Deeply Contradictory Signals as Diplomacy Stalls
detail: As of Day 27 of the conflict, the US and Iran continue to offer contradictory signals on the state of diplomacy. Trump claims the US is 'in negotiations right now' involving Witkoff, Kushner, Rubio, and Vance. Iran's Foreign Minister Araghchi acknowledged message exchanges through mediators but denied it constitutes negotiations. Iran's military spokesperson stated 'No one like us will make a deal with you. Not now. Not ever.' Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey have been pressing for a peace summit in Islamabad. Analysts note the maximum Iran might offer does not meet the US minimum demands — which include full dismantlement of nuclear capabilities and abandonment of proxy networks.

title: US Sends 1,000+ 82nd Airborne Troops to Middle East Amid Continued Operations
detail: At least 1,000 troops from the 82nd Airborne Division were ordered to deploy to the Middle East in the coming days, three sources with knowledge of the plans told the Associated Press, signaling continued US military commitment to the theater. White House Press Secretary Leavitt warned Iran it will be 'hit harder than they have ever been hit before' if it fails to accept the military reality. The escalation comes as Iran-backed Iraqi militias claimed 291 total attacks since the war began.

title: IEA: Hormuz Closure Is 'Greatest Global Energy Security Challenge in History'
detail: The International Energy Agency described the Strait of Hormuz crisis as the 'greatest global energy security challenge in history.' Brent crude remained near $111/bbl, up ~84% year-to-date. Shell and TotalEnergies CEOs issued energy shortage warnings. Slovenia introduced fuel rationing — the first EU country to do so. Spain approved a €5 billion emergency energy aid package. Asian LNG spot prices remain at multi-year highs following QatarEnergy's force majeure declaration. The World Economic Forum warned of cascading food security impacts from fertilizer supply disruption.

title: Trump Issues 48-Hour Hormuz Deadline; Postpones Iranian Energy Infrastructure Strikes
detail: President Trump issued Iran a 48-hour ultimatum to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, simultaneously ordering a 5-day postponement of planned US strikes on Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure — citing ceasefire talks as the reason for the pause. Trump stated he backed off threatening Iranian energy infrastructure 'based on the fact we're negotiating.' He also threatened that if Iran fails to comply it will be 'hit harder than ever before.' The temporary pause was interpreted as a de facto diplomatic signal, though Iran's official position remained defiant.

title: Iran Signals Non-Hostile Vessels May Transit Hormuz Under Iranian Coordination
detail: Iran's UN mission signaled that 'non-hostile vessels' may be allowed to transit the Strait of Hormuz if they coordinate with Iranian maritime authorities in advance. China, India, and Pakistan-flagged tankers have reportedly already been allowed selective passage. The signal caused a brief dip in oil prices. Analysts cautioned it did not represent a genuine reopening and that enforcement was selective and unpredictable. Iran's naval commanders reiterated that US and Israeli-affiliated vessels would still be denied passage.

title: Pakistan, Egypt, Turkey Push for Islamabad Peace Summit
detail: Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey stepped up diplomatic efforts to host a ceasefire summit in Islamabad, with Pakistani PM Sharif declaring the capital 'stands ready' to host negotiations. Turkey's AKP confirmed it is 'passing messages' between Tehran and Washington. Egypt's FM proposed a UN-backed multilateral framework. Neither Iran nor the US formally agreed to participate. Iran's formal diplomatic position remains that it will not negotiate while US-Israeli strikes continue, though back-channel message exchanges through all three countries are acknowledged by both sides.
